commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a one-sentence Windows Corepack EPERM fallback to the Getting Started tip telling users to install pnpm globally through npm.

PR surface: Docs +1. Total +1 across 1 file.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as an OpenClaw runtime bug reproduction. The PR body provides copied Windows terminal output for the Corepack EPERM fallback, but this docs-only review did not replay Windows setup.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A focused docs repair can relocate and reword the fallback without changing runtime behavior or package-manager policy.

target.title: docs: add Windows pnpm fallback for Corepack EPERM
target.body:
Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Windows users can encounter a Corepack "EPERM" permission error when running "corepack enable" during OpenClaw source setup.

Why does this matter now?

The error blocks installation of pnpm through Corepack and can prevent contributors from continuing onboarding or development setup.

What is the intended outcome?

Provide a documented fallback path that allows users to install pnpm directly through npm when Corepack fails.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Runtime behavior changes
- Build system changes
- Installation script changes
- Any code changes outside documentation

What does success look like?

A Windows user encountering a Corepack "EPERM" error can continue setup successfully using the documented fallback.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Accuracy of the Windows-specific guidance
- Whether the fallback recommendation is appropriate for onboarding documentation
- Clarity of the wording for new contributors

Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No.

Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  Windows Corepack "EPERM" failure during pnpm setup.

- Real environment tested:
  Windows 11 using Git Bash with Node.js installed under "C:\Program Files\nodejs".

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

corepack enable
npm install -g pnpm
pnpm -v

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal output):

Internal Error: EPERM: operation not permitted, open 'C:\Program Files\nodejs\pnpm.CMD'
Error: EPERM: operation not permitted, open 'C:\Program Files\nodejs\pnpm.CMD'

changed 1 package in 5s

1 package is looking for funding
  run `npm fund` for details

11.2.2

- Observed result after fix:

Corepack failed with an EPERM permission error, but installing pnpm through npm succeeded. Running "pnpm -v" returned "11.2.2", confirming the fallback path works.

- What was not tested:

OpenClaw runtime execution after installation.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints:

This PR documents a setup workaround and does not modify runtime behavior.

- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

"corepack enable" failed while attempting to create "pnpm.CMD" inside "C:\Program Files\nodejs".

Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

corepack enable
npm install -g pnpm
pnpm -v

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Documentation only.

What failed before this fix, if known?

"corepack enable" failed with an EPERM permission error on Windows.

If no test was added, why not?

This PR only updates documentation and does not change application code.

Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

No.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Documentation accuracy.

How is that risk mitigated?

The guidance is based on a real Windows reproduction and successful validation of the fallback path.

Current review state

What is the next action?

Await maintainer review and automated checks.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Nothing from the author side at this time.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Addressed the Real behavior proof requirement by providing a real Windows reproduction and terminal output evidence.
target.author: africoding

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
